Although it touches governance and social tools, its primary focus is management and conservation of a natural resource — water — so it is distinct from infrastructure, economic, or purely social policies.

Extraction Reasoning: Urban forestry/heat mitigation is separated because the plan provides specific, measurable tree-planting targets and regulatory actions (LVMC and zoning amendments) directly aimed at reducing urban heat island effects — a different mode of adaptation (green infrastructure) than water or parks.
Classification Reasoning: This policy targets urban forestry and green infrastructure to reduce the urban heat island by expanding native/adaptive tree canopy, so its core action is environmental (planting and managing trees) rather than building or economic programs. Although it mentions zoning and LVMC amendments, those are enabling measures; the primary intervention is ecosystem-based (canopy/forestry) which distinguishes it from governance, housing, or social-only policies.

Extraction Reasoning: Parks expansion and walkable access were treated separately because the document prescribes park system planning, acreage targets and walkability goals to provide cooling and resilience benefits — an open-space/cooling strategy that is operationally distinct from tree-planting or flood infrastructure.

 Public facilities are operated from the additional viewpoint of adaptation. 〇 Adapting to the effects of climate change (adaptation)

Adaptation measures by the city promote the measures by all the city organizations as well as promoting voluntary actions by citizens and business operators in order to firmly advance the measures stated in Chapter 6 “Adaptation to the effect of climate change due to global warming (Adaptation Measures).”
Extraction Reasoning: Adaptation‑oriented operation and continuity of public facilities — separated because operating public facilities from an adaptation viewpoint (continuity, use during disasters) is an operational policy distinct from construction or training measures.
Classification Reasoning: This policy was created to mainstream climate adaptation into the operation and continuity of public facilities and to promote coordinated adaptation actions across city organizations and voluntary actions by citizens and businesses. It is separate from infrastructure or environmental resource policies because it focuses on institutionalizing adaptation through planning, inter‑agency coordination and operational continuity rather than on physical construction, resource management, or direct technical interventions.

Policy Name: Urban permeability and creation of interconnected planted water/green areas (Network of oases)
Policy Details: Most urban land is impermeable. This prevents natural water infiltration and contributes to heat islands as well as flooding, which is a particularly undesirable situation given the acceleration of extreme climate events. The City of Geneva will embark on a process of making part of the urban area more permeable and will create a series of new, interconnected planted areas with trees and water.
Extraction Reasoning: Urban permeability and creation of interconnected planted water/green areas (Network of oases): This is an explicit territorial-development adaptation measure addressing impermeability, flooding and urban heat by creating interconnected planted areas and was presented as a clear spatial planning action distinct from other measures.
Classification Reasoning: This policy focuses on restoring natural hydrology and urban ecosystems by increasing permeability and creating interconnected planted/water areas, so it complements but remains distinct from prior policies on built infrastructure or transport. It is classified separately because its primary action is ecological land-use change and water management (green-blue infrastructure and biodiversity), rather than building standards, housing provision, or economic measures.
